title: December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19  | Stats NZ
content:
## December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
05 March 2021, 12:00pm
December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 provides technical users with information about our approach to measuring COVID-19 in the forthcoming release of Gross domestic product: December 2020 quarte r (scheduled for 18 March 2021).
This paper follows earlier method papers that describe the measurement challenges faced due to the nature of the economic shock caused by COVID-19, and how we approached them. See related pages for previous papers.
Key events in the December 2020 quarter Data and methodology considerations Interpreting quarterly, annual and same quarter previous year measures Changes to methods of interpolation without an indicator Summary of approaches for December 2020 quarter gross domestic product

## Key events in the December 2020 quarter
Globally, the COVID-19 pandemic continued, affecting trade and travel flows. New Zealand’s border remained closed to almost all travellers throughout the quarter, with the exception of New Zealand citizens, residents with valid travel conditions, and those granted approval to travel.
The December 2020 quarter started with Auckland in alert level 2 and the rest of New Zealand in alert level 1. On 8 October 2020 Auckland moved to alert level 1, with this alert level in place across the country for the rest of the December 2020 quarter.
Several states in Australia allowed New Zealand travellers from areas marked as ‘non-COVID designated hotspots’ to enter the country without mandatory quarantine during the December 2020 quarter.
The wage subsidy scheme was not in place during the December 2020 quarter.
Overall, economic activity in the December 2020 quarter was less disrupted by moving in and out of domestic COVID-19 containment measures than the preceding two quarters.

## Data and methodology considerations
The abrupt economic shock caused by COVID-19 and measures to contain it posed challenges to our normal methods of measuring economic activity. While the December 2020 quarter will be less affected than the previous two quarters, the measurement of some GDP(P) and GDP(E) components are still impacted and we continue to use alternative data and methodologies where this is the case. See Summary of approaches for December 2020 quarter gross domestic product for further detail.
We used alternative data sources in place of some of our usual data sources and methodologies in the March, June, and September 2020 quarters to more accurately capture quarterly changes in activity. This was applied in the following cases for:
- components with methods that were unable to capture the sharp changes in activity that occurred due to the alert level 4 lockdown (for example, employment-based indicators that were affected by the wage subsidy scheme)
- components that experienced sharp changes in relationships between output and intermediate consumption
- components that have a method of interpolation without a quarterly indicator.
The absence of further sharp shocks to economic activity means we are able to move toward resuming our usual quarterly methods for many components of GDP(P) and GDP(E). During the September 2020 quarter we reverted some components to their usual quarterly method. The same review process will be followed in the December 2020 quarter.
We will compare the level of activity implied by our alternative approach with the level of activity of our usual quarterly method and resume use of our usual method once these are comparable. In practice this involves comparing the difference (in dollar terms) between the alternative indicator and the usual indicator for the current quarter against the average quarterly variation in the usual indicator series. We will resume using the usual indicator once these are comparable.
If they are not comparable we will use the alternative data source and continue to review the series in future quarters. We will have an opportunity to review and align the series once detailed data from the annual national accounts are available.
For example, the normal quarterly indicator for waste collection, treatment, and disposal services is the quarterly employment survey (QES) (released in labour market statistics ). We used sales volumes from the business financial collection (BFC) (formerly published as an experimental series called the business data collection ) as the quarterly indicator for the March and June 2020 quarters. In the September 2020 quarter, the difference between the BFC and QES was similar to the average quarterly variation in the QES series, so we reverted to using the QES to calculate quarterly movement for this industry.
We will endeavour to provide an update on the approach being used for GDP(P) and GDP(E) components on 12 March 2021, ahead of the release of Gross domestic product: December 2020 quarter .
As detailed in our previous papers (see related pages), applying additive outliers to unusual data points ensures that historical seasonal factors will not be adversely affected, and we can continue to produce credible seasonally adjusted results.
We will continue with our use of additive outliers to treat unusual data points. We will be testing all December 2020 quarter results to determine where additive outliers need to be applied. Previous outliers applied to March, June, and/or September 2020 quarter results will be retained in the upcoming release of Gross domestic product: December 2020 quarter .
The December 2020 quarter was characterised by an overall lower level of restriction on activity and a return to more normal conditions compared with the March, June, and September 2020 quarters. As such we expect that there will be fewer unusual movements in components of GDP(P) and GDP(E) compared with those observed over recent quarters.
The availability of additional data points and the absence of further sharp shocks to economic activity mean that we can review and adjust our additive outlier treatment of COVID-19 affected series. We will re-test all series for the March, June, and September 2020 quarters to determine whether to apply new additive outliers, remove those no longer required, and finalise outliers already in place.
We will commence this review shortly and plan to implement changes in the release of Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter .
See Impact of COVID-19 on seasonally adjusted and trend series for further information about Stats NZ’s approach to seasonal adjustment.

## Interpreting quarterly, annual, and same quarter previous year measures
The unusual movements in recent quarters have highlighted that there are several ways of measuring growth and that each measure can show different things. This section outlines the differences between quarterly, average annual, and same quarter previous year growth rates that we publish, and what each measure told us in the September 2020 quarter.
- Quarterly growth is the movement between the current and previous quarters’ seasonally adjusted levels.
- Average annual growth compares the sum of the actual levels of the latest four quarters (including the current quarter) with the sum of the same quarters from the previous year. It is the annual equivalent to the quarterly growth and is what we would publish in a set of annual measurements.
- Same quarter previous year growth compares the actual levels between the current quarter with the same quarter from the previous year.
The main figures that we quote in our information releases are the quarterly and the average annual results.
The economic impacts of COVID-19 mean that it is useful to compare all three measures as each provide different insights into how New Zealand’s economy is tracking.
In Gross domestic product: September 2020 quarter we published a quarterly rise of 14 percent; a same quarter previous year rise of 0.4 percent; and an annual average decline of 2.2 percent. These combined results showed that:
- quarter on quarter activity grew strongly, coming off a large fall in the June 2020 quarter that was affected by the alert level 4 lockdown
- the same quarter previous year result showed that economic activity in the September 2020 quarter reached similar levels seen prior to COVID-19
- the negative average annual result showed that the economy had not yet recouped the losses experienced in the first half of 2020, as the activity over the year to September 2020 was less than that in the previous year.

## Changes to methods of interpolation without an indicator
We reviewed our data sources and methods to identify methods that would be unable to capture the change in activity caused by COVID-19 and efforts to contain it.
During this process we identified inconsistencies in how interpolation methods were being applied. We have standardised the approach and will introduce changes for some components of GDP(P) and GDP(E) in Gross domestic product: December 2020 quarter . This will result in revisions from the June 2019 quarter onwards to the following series.
- Metal ore, non-metallic mineral mining, and quarrying in mining
- Own account capital formation for electricity generation and on-selling, and electricity transmission and distribution in electricity, gas, water, and waste services
- Water transport, other transport, postal and courier services and warehousing and storage in transport, postal, and warehousing services
- Library and other information services in information, media, and telecommunications
- Non-residential property operators in rental, hiring, and real estate services
- Public order, safety and regulator services, local government nonmarket in Central government administration, defence, and public safety
- Consumption of fixed capital in local government expenditure
There is further work in progress to update the method for other components and we intend to implement these changes over the next year.

## Summary of approaches for December 2020 quarter gross domestic product
Below we note the components of GDP production and GDP expenditure where we have deviated from the usual quarterly indicators in the March, June, and September 2020 quarters, and detail the approach that will be used in the December 2020 quarter release. As part of compiling the December 2020 quarter release of gross domestic product we have reviewed the application of the alternative indicators in previous quarters, and as a result, there have been some changes applied. These will contribute to revisions to previously published results. Please note that the information below is valid at date of release and is subject to change. Any further updates will be noted in the release of Gross domestic product: December 2020 quarter on 18 March 2021 .
- Metal ore and non-metallic mineral mining and quarrying normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. Derived value-added data from the BFC was used for the June and September 2020 quarters and will be again in the December 2020 quarter.
Output indicators are normally sourced from value of building work put in place and are directly collected for private-sector heavy and civil construction.
These are the changes for the components of this industry.
- QES is normally used for the public-sector component of heavy and civil engineering construction, while the private sector component uses the movement in the ‘other construction’ category of gross fixed capital formation (GFKF). Derived value-added volumes data from the BFC were used in the June and September 2020 quarters and will be again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Construction services is normally derived from the combined movement in residential and non-residential building construction, which is sourced from ‘value of building work done’ data. BFC data indicates issues in gross output and intermediate consumption ratios, and so derived BFC value-added data was used in June 2020 quarter and September 2020 quarter. We will use the BFC data again for this component in the December 2020 quarter and are currently investigating a permanent change of method.

#### Retail trade, accommodation, and food services
All sub-industries in this industry normally use output indicators sourced from retail trade survey data. The following sub-industries used derived BFC value-added data (including change in stock levels) to account for changes in gross output and intermediate consumption in June 2020 quarter and September 2020 quarter, and will again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Food and beverage services
- Accommodation services

#### Transport, postal, and warehousing services
These are the changes for the components of this industry.
- Air and space transport normally use passenger and freight revenue kilometres data, collected directly from major companies. In the June and September 2020 quarters we used derived BFC value-added volumes data to account for changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption and will again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Scenic and sightseeing transport normally uses international visitor numbers. In the June and September 2020 quarters we used BFC sales volumes data and will do so again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Warehousing and storage services normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In the June 2020 quarter we used BFC sales volumes, and we reverted back to interpolation without an indicator in the September 2020 quarter. Updated data has since been received and as a result we will be applying derived BFC valued-added data to the June, September, and December 2020 quarters.

#### Information, media, and telecommunications
These are the changes for the components of this industry.
- Publishing (except internet and music publishing) normally uses QES. BFC value-added data was used in the June and September 2020 quarters and will be used again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Motion picture and sound recording activities normally uses gross earnings from the employer monthly schedule, which overstated work done in previous quarters due to the impact of the wage subsidy. A composite indicator derived from household labour force survey (HLFS) and employment indicators (EI) data was used in the June and September 2020 quarters and we will use this again in the December 2020 quarter.

#### Professional, scientific, technical, administrative, and support services
These are the changes for the components of this industry.
- Professional scientific and technical services normally uses GST sales data. The derived BFC value-added data was used for the June and September 2020 quarters and will be used again in December 2020 quarter.
- Scientific, architectural, and engineering services normally uses GST sales data from the BFC. BFC value-added data was used in the June 2020 quarter. We have changed September 2020 quarter from GST sales to BFC value-added data and we will revert back to GST sales in the December 2020 quarter.
- Advertising, market research, and management services normally uses BFC sales volumes data. We used BFC value-added data in the June and September 2020 quarters and will again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Travel agency and tour arrangement services normally use QES indicators. In the March 2020 quarter GST sales data was used to represent the change in activity. The derived BFC value-added was used for the June and September 2020 quarters. As this component is still affected by the absence of international tourism in New Zealand, we will use BFC value-added data again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Employment and other administrative services normally use QES. In September 2020 quarter we used BFC value-added data to account for changes in gross output and intermediate consumption ratios and we will use this again in December 2020 quarter.

#### Education and training
All education and training sub-industries normally use a method of interpolation of the annual (without a quarterly indicator). The following approaches will be used to represent activity in this industry.
- Preschool education was estimated in the March 2020 quarter using an assumption of no activity for the days of the alert level 4 lockdown, and 100 percent activity at alert level 1. For the June and September 2020 quarters we used cumulative attendance hours. For the December 2020 quarter we are likely to revert to the usual method of interpolation of the annual with no indicator.
- Tertiary education was estimated using student visa data in the March, June, and September 2020 quarters and will be again in December 2020 quarter.
- Adult, community, and other education was adjusted using unit-level information to estimate activity during alert level 4. In the June and September 2020 quarters, BFC sales data was used and will be again in the December 2020 quarter.

#### Healthcare and social assistance
The following sub-industries normally use QES indicators. The following approaches will be used to measure activity in this industry.
- The market component of hospital services used BFC sales volumes in the March, June, and September 2020 quarters, and will again in the December 2020 quarter.
- The market component of private medical and other health care services used BFC sales volumes in the March 2020 quarter. A composite indicator derived from low-level HLFS data and monthly EI data was used in the June and September 2020 quarters, and will be again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Residential care services and social assistance used BFC sales volumes in the June and September 2020 quarters, and will again in the December 2020 quarter.

#### Arts, recreation, and other services
The following approaches will be used to measure activity in this industry.
- Heritage and artistic activities used BFC sales volumes in the June and September 2020 quarters and will again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Sport and recreation activities used BFC sales volumes in the June and September 2020 quarters and will again in December 2020 quarter.
- Religious services used a composite indicator derived from low-level HLFS data and monthly EI data in the June and September 2020 quarters. We will revert to using QES in the December 2020 quarter.
- Civic, professional, and other interest groups used derived value-added from BFC in the June and September 2020 quarters and will again in the December 2020 quarter.
In cases where changes have been made to line up to corresponding components on the production side of GDP, the different underlying concepts between measurement of production and expenditure have been accounted for.

#### Non-profit organisations serving households
The normal method is interpolation without an indicator. For the June 2020 quarter, we used employee counts for non-profit organisations serving households, by industry, as weights. These were then multiplied by quarterly movements in these industries observed on the production side. We reverted to the usual method in the September 2020 quarter.

#### Household consumption expenditure
- For household consumption expenditure (HCE) on education, domestic expenditure was aligned with the approaches used for GDP(P) education sub-industries in the March, June, and September 2020 quarters, and will be again in the December 2020 quarter. The measurement of education travel credits is consistent with the approach used in our balance of payments.
- Hospital services and out-patient services were adjusted to align with health and social assistance, using BFC sales values data.
- Recreational services and cultural services both normally use QES indicators, in line with heritage and artistic activities and sport and recreation activities in the arts and recreation industry. These sub-industries used BFC sales values in the March, June, and September 2020 quarters and will again in the December 2020 quarter.
- Road passenger services and other passenger transport costs were both adjusted using electronic card transactions (ECT) data in March 2020 quarter. Low level BFC sales values data was used in the June and September 2020 quarters, and this will be used again in the December 2020 quarter.
- We updated travel credit ratios to several categories in the June 2020 quarter and have maintained these ratios in the September and December 2020 quarters.

#### Gross fixed capital formation
We will assess whether we can revert to using the usual method in the December 2020 quarter for the components listed below.
The local government component of other construction uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In the March 2020 quarter an adjustment was made to account for the days in alert level 4 lockdown. In the June 2020 and September 2020 quarters, sales data from the BFC was used and will be again in the December 2020 quarter.
Parts of the private and central government components of other construction use interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In the June 2020 and September 2020 quarters, BFC sales data was used, and will be again in the December 2020 quarter
Land improvements uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In the June 2020 and September 2020 quarters we used low-level movements from other construction to represent this component and this will be used again in the December 2020 quarter
Tourist spending in New Zealand (used in exports of travel services and also in the calculation of household consumption expenditure) is normally estimated using data collected in the IVS. IVS data collection has ceased as a result of border closures introduced from March 2020. A new method for measuring international tourist expenditure has been developed to fill this gap. See Estimating visitor expenditure in New Zealand during the June 2020 quarter for further information about this new methodology.
For exports of education services, we estimate the tuition fees paid by overseas students from Export Education Levy (EEL) data compiled by the Ministry of Education (MoE).
For the March, June, and September 2020 quarters, we used updated 2019 EEL data from MoE and adjusted it using updated student visa numbers from Immigration New Zealand. See International trade June 2020 quarter data sources and methods . The same approach will be used for December 2020 quarter.
